claims.evidence_text: Freezing events that occur after germination cause the winter wheat to go through a vernalization process that is needed to promote flowering in the spring.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat requires a vernalization process (post-germination freezing) to promote spring flowering.

claims.evidence_text: The N requirements for a winter wheat crop roughly starts with 1.75 pounds of N for each bushel of wheat. A 60-bushel wheat crop requires 105 total pounds of N per acre.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat N requirement is roughly 1.75 lb N per bushel (60-bu crop needs 105 lb N/acre).

claims.evidence_text: A 60-bushel wheat crop requires 105 total pounds of N per acre.
claims.claim_summary: A 60-bushel winter wheat crop requires 105 lb N per acre.

claims.evidence_text: If a soil test is not available... then a minimum of 30 pounds N per acre applied pre-plant or at planting should be enough to supply those early plant needs.
claims.claim_summary: A minimum of 30 lb N/acre pre-plant supplies early fall growth and tillering needs.

claims.evidence_text: A top-dress application, when applied after the crop greens up but before it starts to joint (Feekes 4-5)... N applied after the crop starts to joint (Feekes 6), often has limited yield response unless the crop is severely deficient in N.
claims.claim_summary: Top-dress N before jointing (Feekes 4-5); N after jointing (Feekes 6) has limited yield response.

claims.evidence_text: In a no-till wheat production system, if liquid N is applied to the soil surface, it can be tied up... a top-dress application of an additional 20 to 30 pounds of N per acre, prior to the rapid plant growth in the spring is often beneficial.
claims.claim_summary: No-till wheat benefits from an extra 20-30 lb N/acre to offset residue tie-up of surface N.

claims.evidence_text: Phosphorus (P) is essential for root development, tillering and resistance to winterkill. Since it is a very immobile nutrient in the soil it is important to place the P fertilizer in the soil at a depth where wheat roots can reach it.
claims.claim_summary: P is essential for root development, tillering, and winterkill resistance; place in soil within root reach.

claims.evidence_text: Potassium (K) should also be applied before planting if soil test levels are low. Many soils have enough K levels in the soil to support a high yielding winter wheat crop.
claims.claim_summary: Apply K before planting if soil test is low; many soils have adequate K for high-yield wheat.

claims.evidence_text: A crop exhibiting these symptoms as it is breaking dormancy and greening up, can benefit from a base application of ten pounds of S in an early top-dress fertilizer application.
claims.claim_summary: Sulfur-deficient wheat benefits from a base application of 10 lb S/acre in early top-dress.

claims.evidence_text: if the crop is being grown on a sandy or sandy loam soil with less than 3% organic matter, this along with above normal precipitation, can reveal a S deficiency.
claims.claim_summary: S deficiency in wheat appears on sandy soils with under 3% organic matter and high precipitation.

claims.evidence_text: A fertile, deep, loam soil with good internal drainage is often considered the best soil to grow a wheat crop... one soil type, classified as a peat soil, which can have elevated levels of iron, sodium, and manganese, has been identified as unfavorable for wheat production.
claims.claim_summary: Best soil for wheat is fertile deep loam with good internal drainage; peat soil is unfavorable.

claims.evidence_text: A fertile, deep, loam soil with good internal drainage is often considered the best soil to grow a wheat crop.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat grows best on soil with good internal drainage.

claims.evidence_text: The optimum soil pH for wheat production is between 5.5 and 7.5.
claims.claim_summary: Optimum soil pH for wheat production is between 5.5 and 7.5.

claims.evidence_text: Growing wheat in acidic soils with a pH of less than 5.5, can lead to increased solubility of aluminum, manganese, and iron which can be toxic to the plant and reduce plant growth and yield potential.
claims.claim_summary: Below pH 5.5, aluminum/manganese/iron toxicity reduces wheat growth and yield.

claims.evidence_text: Winter wheat produced on alkaline soils, with a pH of 7.5 or higher, can cause a reduced availability of certain micronutrients such as iron, zinc, and manganese.
claims.claim_summary: On alkaline soils pH 7.5 or higher, iron/zinc/manganese availability is reduced for wheat.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat has above average soil salinity tolerance. This means that a soil with up to an EC of 6.0 will not have a yield effect on wheat crop
claims.claim_summary: Wheat yield is unaffected by soil EC up to 6.0 dS/m.

claims.evidence_text: When growing a wheat crop in a soil with a salinity of 13.0, an expected relative yield decreases of 50% can be expected.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat shows a 50% relative yield decrease at soil salinity (EC) of 13.0 dS/m.

claims.evidence_text: Wheat has above average soil salinity tolerance.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat has above-average soil salinity tolerance.

claims.evidence_text: Not taking residual N into consideration can lead to overfertilization which increases production expenses and cause lodging problems prior to harvest, reducing yield potential.
claims.claim_summary: Over-fertilization with N can cause wheat lodging prior to harvest, reducing yield.

claims.evidence_text: In a no-till wheat production system, if liquid N is applied to the soil surface, it can be tied up, or immobilized, on the high amounts of crop residue. This N that is tied up will not be immediately available to the growing crop
claims.claim_summary: In no-till wheat, surface-applied N is immobilized on crop residue and not immediately available.
